svg.title: Two-pointer skip-check trace
svg.desc: Step-by-step trace of a two-pointer algorithm that checks whether string s can become a subsequence of string t after skipping (deleting) exactly one character of s, using s = apple and t = appitite, showing the forward pass building l[], the backward pass building r[], the scan for a valid skip point, and the final matched/skipped mapping.
svg.text: goal: can s become a subsequence of t if we're allowed to skip (delete) exactly one character of s?
Phase 1 — forward pass: l[i+1] = leftmost t-index matching s[i]
i=0 (s[0]='a'): j starts at 0 → t[0]='a' is an immediate match → l[1] = 0, j advances to 1
i=1 (s[1]='p'): immediate match at t[1] → l[2] = 1, j → 2
i=2 (s[2]='p'): immediate match at t[2] → l[3] = 2, j → 3
i=3 (s[3]='l'): scan forward from j=3 looking for 'l' — every position mismatches:

g.text: j reaches m = 8 → break; l[4] and l[5] are never set

svg.text: resulting l[] array (∅ = never computed, stays at the initial sentinel):
l[n] = l[5] = ∅ → s is not already a subsequence of t → continue to phase 2
Phase 2 — backward pass: r[i] = rightmost t-index matching s[i], scanning from the end
i=4 (s[4]='e'): j starts at m-1=7 → t[7]='e' is an immediate match → r[4] = 7, j advances to 6
i=3 (s[3]='l'): scan backward from j=6 looking for 'l' — every position mismatches:

g.text: j drops below 0 → break; r[3], r[2], r[1], r[0] are never set

svg.text: resulting r[] array (r[5] = m = 8 was the initial boundary, set before the loop even ran):
r[0..3] keep their default -1 — the loop broke before ever writing them
Phase 3 — for each i, can s[i] be skipped? check l[i] + 1 < r[i+1]
i=4 is never reached — the function already returned true at i=3
How it fits together — skip s[3]='l', match the rest
green = matched prefix · teal = matched suffix · amber = spare room in t · red = the one character of s we skip
